entities.description: A 2003 electronic text of the 1818 title page, anonymous Advertisement, and chronicle on printed pages 5-29. It retains Pinkerton-derived editorial notes and the Add MS 5467 lacuna, but its header compresses the intervening Pinkerton and 1818 print layers into a direct manuscript-source statement.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item deathjas
notes: The PROFILEDESC declares Latin, but the encoded work is predominantly Middle English and includes one brief Middle French maxim; Latin belongs to the lost source relationship rather than the encoded language inventory.
The BODY contains 31 prose paragraphs and one four-line closing stanza.

entities.description: The anonymous Middle English prose account of James I of Scotland's murder in 1437 and the punishment of the conspirators, translated from a now-lost Latin narrative by John Shirley and distinguished here from its witnesses, editions, encodings, and print paratexts.
entities.label: The Dethe of the Kynge of Scotis (abstract Middle English work)

rights.basis: The work was translated and copied in the fifteenth century; no modern authorship is involved in the underlying medieval text.
rights.component: The medieval Middle English chronicle as an abstract textual work

bibliographic.citation: The Dethe of James Kynge of Scotis, in Miscellanea Scotica: A Collection of Tracts Relating to the History, Antiquities, Topography, and Literature of Scotland, vol. II (Glasgow: printed for and sold by John Wylie & Co.; R. Chapman, printer, 1818), separately paginated pp. 1-29.
bibliographic.pages: separately paginated pp. 1-29; historical text pp. 5-29
bibliographic.publisher: John Wylie & Co.; R. Chapman, printer
bibliographic.title: Miscellanea Scotica: A Collection of Tracts Relating to the History, Antiquities, Topography, and Literature of Scotland

entities.description: The immediate printed source of the U-M encoding: a reset of Pinkerton's Appendix XIII with a new title page and anonymous Advertisement. The Advertisement says the tract first appeared in Pinkerton and disputes his claim that the lost Latin account was published in Scotland by authority.
entities.label: Anonymous Glasgow reset in Miscellanea Scotica, volume II (1818)
notes: The title leaf's manuscript date MCCCCXL repeats Pinkerton's inference from a date attached to another Shirley translation; it is not treated as a demonstrated date for Add MS 5467 or for the translation.

rights.basis: The anonymous edition was published in 1818; ordinary copyright and edition-layout terms expired long before 2026.
rights.component: The anonymous 1818 reset, title page, Advertisement, and published typography

bibliographic.citation: John Pinkerton, The History of Scotland from the Accession of the House of Stuart to That of Mary, with Appendixes of Original Papers, vol. I (London: C. Dilly, 1797), Appendix XIII, pp. 462-475.
bibliographic.title: The History of Scotland from the Accession of the House of Stuart to That of Mary, with Appendixes of Original Papers

entities.description: The first identified publication of the chronicle, printed as Appendix XIII on pages 462-475. Pinkerton explicitly identifies his source as British Library Add MS 5467, marks the torn-leaf gap, reports the Shirley colophon, and adds historical and lexical notes.
entities.label: John Pinkerton, The History of Scotland, volume I (1797), Appendix XIII

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1797 and John Pinkerton died in 1826; ordinary copyright terms expired long before 2026.
rights.component: Pinkerton's 1797 published edition and editorial notes

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: The catalog's composite-volume range and internal clue do not establish an exact date for this item's copying.
dateStatements.value: Mid-to-late fifteenth century; the composite volume's internal reference to Henry VI as formerly king suggests part was copied after 1461

entities.description: The defective manuscript witness directly transcribed by Pinkerton. A torn leaf between ff. 72 and 73 causes the gap inherited by the 1797 publication, 1818 reset, U-M encoding, and repository artifact; a fragment numbered f. 72* preserves part of the lost leaf.

holding.notes: The chronicle is item 6 at ff. 72v-84v, with fragment f. 72*.

entities.label: British Library, Add MS 5467, ff. 72v-84v and fragment f. 72*
notes: The closing colophon identifies John Shirley as translator from Latin in his last age.
No public full digital facsimile was verified on 2026-07-15.

physicalDescription.extent: 224 folios plus fragment f. 72*; chronicle on ff. 72v-84v
notes: The composite manuscript contains several texts and hands.
The catalog tentatively associates the hand across ff. 23-224 with Rouland Brugge; this is not converted into certain scribal attribution.

entities.description: A complete later copy of the chronicle. George Neilson used ff. 14-15 to print the passage missing from Add MS 5467 and also printed fuller concluding material; its date remains disputed between the current NLS sixteenth-century cataloging and Neilson's description of an unidentified seventeenth-century hand.

holding.notes: Neilson locates the complete chronicle at ff. 13-25 and the missing Add MS 5467 passage principally at ff. 14-15.

next_steps: Request written clarification from U-M Library that separately addresses copyright status, provider terms, nonprofit redistribution, commercial redistribution, and reuse of the encoding and markup.
Until clarified, cite both the current NoC-US label and the embedded restriction rather than selecting whichever is more permissive.
openQuestions.question: Does U-M consider the restrictive redistribution statement embedded in the 2003 XML superseded by the current No Copyright-United States label, and may the XML encoding and markup be redistributed in a commercial or nonprofit corpus?

next_steps: Consult Matheson's full manuscript and stemmatic discussion and later paleographical work.
Ask NLS for the basis of its current sixteenth-century date and whether the hand has been reassessed since Neilson's seventeenth-century description.
Compare complete witness texts directly when full facsimiles or permitted reproductions become available; do not infer a direct copying relation beforehand.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact relationship among Add MS 38690, Add MS 5467, and Adv.MS.17.1.22, and should the NLS copy be dated to the sixteenth or seventeenth century?

next_steps: Ask BL reference services about Add MS 5467 and Add MS 38690, including any legacy microfilm or unindexed photography and the distinction between reference and reproduction images.
Ask NLS about existing photography or microfilm of Adv.MS.17.1.22 before ordering new images.
If ordering, specify exact folios, image resolution, color target, file format, delivery rights, publication rights, and whether images may be redistributed with the corpus.
Record supplied files and item-specific terms without generalizing from institution-wide copyright pages.
openQuestions.question: Do BL or NLS hold unindexed manuscript photography, legacy microfilm, or other digital surrogates for the three witnesses, and what exact research and reuse terms would apply?

next_steps: Consult Matheson's full source, recipient, and treatment chapters and later scholarship.
Keep the source as an unidentified related work rather than assigning an author, Scottish official-publication history, or physical witness without direct evidence.
Keep circa 1440 as a traditional approximate translation date rather than an exact date proven by the 1818 title page.
openQuestions.question: Who composed the lost Latin narrative, where and when was it written, what exemplar did Shirley translate, and what was the translation's original recipient and purpose?

relations.subjectLabel: Lister M. Matheson, Death and Dissent: Two Fifteenth-Century Chronicles (1999), part I

reviewNotes: The lost Latin narrative is an intellectual source named by the Shirley colophon, not a surviving physical witness; its author, title, exemplar, and transmission remain unknown.
The three manuscript witnesses are represented independently. No direct witness-to-witness relation or stemma is asserted.
The title page's MCCCCXL claim is retained as an 1818/Pinkerton editorial dating claim, not converted into a demonstrated exact date for the translation or Add MS 5467.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is the pinned General Corpus copy of University of Michigan CME item deathjas, a 2003 encoding of an anonymous Glasgow reset printed in Miscellanea Scotica, volume II (1818). That reset reproduces John Pinkerton's first publication in The History of Scotland, volume I (1797), Appendix XIII, which directly transcribes the defective witness British Library Add MS 5467, ff. 72v-84v and fragment f. 72*. The other complete witnesses, British Library Add MS 38690 and National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.17.1.22, are collateral witnesses rather than ancestors of the encoded text. George Neilson's 1904 article prints material from the NLS copy, while Lister M. Matheson's 1999 edition uses Add MS 38690 as base text and collates Add MS 5467. No public full facsimile of any of the three manuscripts was verified on 2026-07-15; the repository catalogs, onsite consultation, and reproduction-order routes are recorded instead. Current U-M No Copyright-United States labeling conflicts with the restrictive redistribution statement embedded in the 2003 XML, and image-reuse terms for the cached third-party scans remain source-specific.

catalogingSubject.label: The Dethe of the Kynge of Scotis
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English prose chronicle of James I of Scotland's murder in 1437 and the punishment of the conspirators, translated from a now-lost Latin narrative by John Shirley. The repository encoding follows a defective Add MS 5467-derived print tradition and includes an 1818 title page and anonymous Advertisement; those two print paratexts are retained as zero-count content parts but are not collapsed into the medieval work. The record excludes the lost Latin narrative as a separate work, the three known manuscript witnesses as physical objects, Pinkerton's 1797 transcription, the anonymous 1818 reset, the U-M encoding, and the checked-in XML artifact.

assertion.notes: The bounds follow the narrated 1437 murder and the named translator's death. Circa 1440 is the current BL date for the complete Add MS 38690 text and a longstanding scholarly date, not a demonstrated exact translation year.

assertion.notes: This is the current catalog date of the complete surviving witness text, not proof that the witness is Shirley's autograph or that translation and copying were simultaneous.

dateStatements.display: Circa 1440 in British Library Add MS 38690
First known publication by John Pinkerton in 1797

extent.description: One prose chronicle represented by 31 BODY paragraphs, followed near its close by one four-line stanza and then the translator colophon. The sections value of 2 counts the XML's two DIV1 containers: the FRONT Advertisement and the BODY chronicle; it does not assert two intrinsic sections of the medieval work, and the separate TITLEPAGE is not a DIV1. The two zero-count FRONT paratext parts comprise that title page and the two-paragraph Advertisement. The encoding has 25 BODY page milestones for printed pp. 5-29, with p. 27 mislabeled as a second p. 26.

formStatements.description: A continuous prose chronicle with a brief four-line verse close. The encoded BODY contains 31 P elements and one LG of four L elements; the closing stanza is modeled as a zero-count nested epilogue rather than changing the work's primary prose form.

assertion.notes: The XML header declares only Latin, but direct inspection shows a Middle English work; Latin names the lost source language and is not an intrinsic language of the surviving translated text.
This is a short embedded quotation, not a sustained second-language section.

scope.description: The brief Jean de Meun maxim on the 1818 title page and in the chronicle's closing counsel

notes: John Shirley is modeled as translator, not author; the author and physical exemplar of the lost Latin narrative remain unknown.

Add MS 5467 is the direct manuscript source of Pinkerton's 1797 publication, which is the textual source of the anonymous 1818 reset; the XML header's direct manuscript wording therefore compresses two print layers.
Add MS 38690 and Adv.MS.17.1.22 are complete collateral witnesses, not direct sources of the defective encoded text.
The 1818 Advertisement and title page remain visible as zero-count paratext so that the encoded boundary is documented without turning edition-level claims into medieval work facts.
The record is partial because the Latin source, translation circumstances, exact witness stemma, and NLS witness date remain unresolved; the encoded content structure itself is completely mapped.

next_steps: Review post-Matheson scholarship for proposed Latin analogues, political context, recipient, and translation date.
Keep the source as an unidentified related work rather than assigning an author, Scottish official publication, or physical witness without direct evidence.
Separate the date of Add MS 38690 from the translation date unless paleographic or textual evidence directly joins them.
openQuestions.question: Who composed the lost Latin narrative, where and when was it written, and what exact Latin exemplar did John Shirley translate?

next_steps: Consult Matheson's full manuscript and stemmatic discussion and later paleographic work.
Ask the National Library of Scotland for the basis of its current sixteenth-century date and whether the script has been reassessed since Neilson's seventeenth-century description.
Compare the complete texts directly when full witness facsimiles or permitted reproductions become available.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact relationship among Add MS 38690, Add MS 5467, and Adv.MS.17.1.22, and should the NLS copy be dated to the sixteenth or seventeenth century?

next_steps: Treat any supplied or parallel text as a separately authorized encoding change, not a metadata correction.
Choose an explicit edition and witness policy before combining readings from different witnesses.
Retain the present manifest's description of the checked-in defective version until such a project is completed.
openQuestions.question: Should a future textual-encoding project supply or parallel the passage missing from the Add MS 5467-derived XML using Add MS 38690 or Adv.MS.17.1.22?

assertion.notes: Add MS 5467 was produced in England, Shirley is associated with London, and the 1818 Advertisement argues from internal evidence for an English writer. No inspected source fixes the translation place or the Latin source's place of composition.

scope.description: The Middle English translation and its English transmission milieu, not the lost Latin narrative's proven origin
Blackfriars at Perth as the principal murder setting

assertion.notes: The explicit translator colophon survives in Add MS 5467 and its Pinkerton-derived print descendants; Add MS 38690 and Adv.MS.17.1.22 lack that colophon.

scope.description: Middle English translation of the lost Latin narrative, not authorship of that source

subjects.label: Murder of James I of Scotland and its aftermath
Punishment, torture, and execution of the conspirators

assertion.notes: The chronicle treats James's captivity, accession, governance, resistance to royal tyranny, murder, and the political claims surrounding the conspirators.

subjects.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy

summaries.value: A contemporary or near-contemporary Middle English prose account of James I of Scotland's early life and English captivity, his return and government, the conspiracy against him, his murder at Blackfriars in Perth in February 1437, and the capture, torture, and execution of the conspirators. The closing counsel warns rulers against avarice and ties lordship to the people's love. John Shirley's Add MS 5467 colophon presents the work as his translation from a lost Latin narrative.

The repository XML encodes the 1818 tract's title page, a two-paragraph Advertisement on printed p. 3, and the historical text on printed pp. 5-29. The Advertisement and title page are edition-level framing; the cataloged work begins with Robert Steward and ends with Shirley's translator colophon. The text inherits the leaf-loss gap of Add MS 5467 even though complete readings survive in Add MS 38690 and Adv.MS.17.1.22.

The full XML has 2 DIV1, 37 P, 1 LG, 4 L, 27 PB, and 8 NOTE1 elements. Four P belong to the HEADER, two to the FRONT Advertisement, and 31 to the BODY chronicle. FRONT has two PB and BODY has 25. The second BODY milestone labeled p. 26 corresponds to printed p. 27 in the inspected 1818 scan; the header's Latin declaration and blanket Shirley authorship are not accepted as intrinsic work metadata.

assertion.notes: Matheson's modern edition and the encoded body heading use the singular Kynge form; Connolly's article title supplies the variant plural Kings.

publication.embeddedTitle: HERE FOLOWING BEGYNNYTHE A FULL LAMENTABLE CRONYCLE, OF THE DETHE AND FALSE MURDURE OF JAMES STEWARDE, KYNG OF SCOTYS, NOUGHT LONG AGONE PRISONER YN ENG- LANDE, THE TYMES OF THE KYNGS HENRYE THE FIFTE AND HENRYE THE SIXTE. — Princes shuld take hede, and draw it to thare memorie, of Maistre Johanes de Moigne counsell, thus said yn Frenche langage, II nest pas sires de son pays, quy de son peple n'est amez. — FROM A MANUSCRIPT WRITTEN IN MCCCCXL.

work.summary: A contemporary or near-contemporary Middle English prose account of James I of Scotland's early life and English captivity, his return and government, the conspiracy against him, his murder at Blackfriars in Perth in February 1437, and the capture, torture, and execution of the conspirators. The closing counsel warns rulers against avarice and ties lordship to the people's love. John Shirley's Add MS 5467 colophon presents the work as his translation from a lost Latin narrative.
